region_of_origin: Nanxiang, Shanghai, China (mid-19th century); widely served in Singapore at Din Tai Fung and Shanghainese restaurants
food_subgroup: Steamed filled dumplings — pork and aspic soup filling in thin wheat wrapper; dim sum / snack category

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Xiaolongbao as a prepared dish. No exact FoodOn term for steamed soup-filled dumpling with aspic filling. Closest parent would be a generic steamed dumpling concept, which is not formally coded in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0GZD used as closest parent for steamed filled dumpling. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Xiaolongbao specifically.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for Xiaolongbao as a prepared food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 6 pieces (approximately 120g) served in a bamboo steamer, as is typical at Din Tai Fung and Shanghainese restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Xiaolongbao — Component Calculation derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Pork, fresh, ground, raw (FDC ID 168907) and wheat dumpling wrapper analogues
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived by component calculation using USDA SR Legacy data for pork mince, wheat dumpling skin, and pork aspic stock. No single USDA entry exists for Xiaolongbao as a prepared dish. Values represent a weighted composite of approximately 55% wheat wrapper, 35% pork mince filling, and 10% aspic/broth by weight, consistent with Din Tai Fung restaurant-standard preparation. Sodium reflects soy-seasoned filling; values cross-checked against Singapore HPB Foodo database for comparable steamed dim sum items.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of wheat-based steamed dumplings — analogous estimate based on steamed wheat dumpling skin GI data; fat and protein from pork filling moderates overall GI
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Xiaolongbao in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of approximately 55 is estimated based on: (1) steamed wheat-based dumpling wrappers have a GI in the range 50–60 per analogous steamed wheat products in the Sydney GI Database; (2) the substantial pork fat (~10.8g/100g) and protein (~11.7g/100g) content of the filling is well-established to attenuate postprandial glycaemic response, lowering effective GI below that of the wrapper alone. GL per serving = GI × (carbohydrate per serving / 100) = 55 × (30g / 100) = 16.5. This is classified as low GI (≤55) and moderate GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content — approximately 640mg per 6-piece serving (533mg/100g × 1.2), rising to approximately 740mg if dipping sauce (Chinkiang black vinegar with soy) is used
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Xiaolongbao to one bamboo steamer (6 pieces) per meal and avoid or minimise the soy-based dipping sauce, as the combined sodium load of approximately 640–740mg represents 28–32% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2000mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycaemic load (GL ~16.5 per 6-piece serving) with GI ~55 attenuated by pork fat and protein in the filling
type2_diabetes.guidance: Six pieces of Xiaolongbao (120g, GL ~16.5) is a reasonable portion for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed as part of a balanced dim sum meal; the pork protein and fat content moderates postprandial blood glucose rise compared to plain wheat-based foods, but portion control remains important given the 30g carbohydrate load per serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~4.2g per 6-piece serving) and sodium (~640mg per serving) are the primary cardiovascular risk factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Xiaolongbao contains approximately 4.2g saturated fat and 640mg sodium per 6-piece serving; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to one steamer basket per meal, avoid the soy dipping sauce, and balance with lower-sodium, lower-saturated-fat foods across the day, as the saturated fat represents approximately 19% of the AHA recommended daily limit of 22g.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Moderate sodium and saturated fat; ginger in filling provides minor gingerol anti-inflammatory contribution
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Xiaolongbao is nutritionally acceptable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis at typical restaurant consumption (6 pieces); ginger in the filling provides gingerol with minor anti-inflammatory properties, though the collagen gelatin content (~2g per basket) is below the therapeutic dose investigated for joint health.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Xiaolongbao (小笼包), commonly known as Shanghai soup dumplings or XLB, are a world-renowned Shanghainese dim sum delicacy originating from Nanxiang, Shanghai, in the mid-19th century. Each dumpling consists of a thin, hand-pleated wheat flour wrapper (traditionally 18 or more folds) encasing a seasoned pork mince filling and a pocket of hot, savoury soup broth. The signature soup is created by incorporating pork aspic (gelatinised concentrated pork stock) into the raw filling; during steaming, the aspic melts into a rich, collagen-rich broth that fills the interior. Xiaolongbao are steamed in small bamboo baskets (xiao long) and served immediately. They are best eaten by gently biting a small hole in the wrapper, releasing steam, sipping the broth from a spoon, and then consuming the whole dumpling with Chinkiang black vinegar and julienned ginger. Nutritionally, a standard serving of 6 pieces (~120g) provides approximately 310 kcal, 14g protein, 13g fat (4.2g saturated), and 30g carbohydrate, with ~640mg sodium. The GI is estimated at ~55 (low), with a glycaemic load of ~16.5 per serving, moderated by the fat and protein content of the pork filling. Clinically, sodium is the primary concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease; the moderate GL makes this a reasonable choice for Type 2 Diabetes when portion-controlled. SCALDING SAFETY NOTE: the soup filling reaches 70–80°C during steaming — always bite a small hole first to release steam before eating. Made globally famous by Din Tai Fung (founded Taipei 1958), with Singapore branches at ION Orchard, VivoCity, Marina Bay Sands, Paragon, and Changi Terminal 3. Source: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component calculation (adapted). Confidence: medium.
common_user_queries: how many calories in soup dumplings
din tai fung soup dumpling nutrition facts

confidence_reason: Nutrient values derived by component calculation from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for pork mince and wheat dumpling wrapper analogues; no single approved-source entry exists for Xiaolongbao as a prepared dish. GI is an analogous estimate based on steamed wheat products from the University of Sydney GI Database, moderated by pork fat and protein content. Cross-checked against Singapore HPB Foodo for comparable steamed dim sum items. Medium confidence is appropriate given the component-calculation methodology and absence of a direct laboratory analysis of this specific dish.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite steamed dumpling preparation is not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2026-04-14. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this specific steamed preparation is not reported in the component sources used. 3. trans_fat_g is confirmed 0.0 per USDA SR Legacy pork and wheat flour component data — no industrial trans fats present in traditional preparation. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed 0.0 — pork mince and wheat wrapper contain negligible vitamin D per USDA SR Legacy. 5. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Xiaolongbao exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-14. 6. Sodium value (533mg/100g) reflects soy-seasoned pork filling only; dipping sauce (Chinkiang black vinegar + soy) adds approximately 80–100mg sodium per serving if used. 7. Portion data: the canonical serving for this record is 6 pieces (~120g) per bamboo steamer, consistent with Din Tai Fung restaurant standard. 8. Halal status: standard Xiaolongbao contains pork and is not halal. 9. Allergens: wheat (gluten) and soy are present; no dairy, egg, nut, or shellfish allergens in standard preparation.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Added Hindi local name, restructured health_context with type2_diabetes key and cardiovascular_disease, mapped per_100g to template field names, set -1 sentinels with notes documentation, restructured gi_source as object, expanded natural_language_description.
Iteration 2: Ensured all aliases_by_language string values present in flat aliases array; added 'Xiǎolóngbāo' deduplication check.

Iteration 3 (this iteration): Validator reported q8 failure — aliases_by_language entries missing from flat aliases. Root cause identified: 'शाओ लोंग बाओ (शंघाई सूप डम्पलिंग)' (the combined Hindi form used in local_names.hindi) was present in aliases_by_language.hindi but absent from flat aliases. 'Xiǎo Lóng Bāo' (romanized) was in aliases_by_language.romanized but absent from flat aliases. Both added to flat aliases array. Full exhaustive cross-check performed: every string value in every aliases_by_language language key verified present verbatim in flat aliases before output.
